commentaries.author: Cornelius a Lapide
commentaries.content: And He had took him, and touched him, and ley him go (ГЋВµГЊвЂ°ГЏв‚¬ГЋВ№ГЋВ»ГЋВ±ГЋВІГЏЕ’ГЋВјГЋВµГЋВЅГЋВїГЏвЂљ, "when he had ouched him. " apprehensum, Vulg. ) He heals by His touch the dropsical man who, from fear of the Pharisees, did not ask to be healed on account of the Sabbath, but only stood up, that when Jesus beheld him He might have compassion on him and heal him. Gregory (lib. ) observes: "The sick of the dropsy is healed in the Pharisee"s presence, for by the bodily infirmity of the one is expressed the mental disease, i. the avarice and covetousness, of the other. " "For," says Bede, "the dropsical man represents one who is weighed down by an overflowing stream of carnal pleasures. Augustine adds, "We, lightly compare one sick of the dropsy to a covetous rich Prayer of Manasseh , who, the more he abounds in riches, the more ardently desires them. Avarice and covetousness, then, are very similar to the dropsy, and as this dire disease is best remedied by abstaining from drinking, so the remedy for unlawful desire is mortification, abstinence, and continence, all of which wither and drive out virtuous habits.

methodology.step_1: Parse main page with BeautifulSoup for structure
methodology.step_3: Use Firecrawl to get full content from 'Go to Commentary' links
methodology.step_4: Fallback to BeautifulSoup for accordion content if needed
advantages: Records verses even without commentaries for complete tracking
